questions.question: What are microservices and how do they differ from a monolith?
questions.answer:
Microservices is an architectural style where an application is structured as a collection of small, independently deployable services — each running in its own process, communicating via well-defined APIs (HTTP/REST, gRPC, messaging).

Monolith vs Microservices:

AspectMonolithMicroservices
DeploymentOne unit deployed togetherEach service deployed independently
ScalingScale the whole appScale only hot services
TechnologyOne language/frameworkPolyglot (each service chooses)
Team structureShared codebaseOne team owns one service
FailureOne bug can bring down everythingFailure is isolated to a service
ComplexitySimple to develop/debugDistributed systems complexity

In CS_PM_Service, I worked on a .NET 6 microservices setup with 8 workers and separate services. Each service had its own DB, deployed independently. Complexity was high — network calls, service discovery, distributed tracing — but each team could deploy independently without coordinating releases.

followUps.question: When should you NOT use microservices?
followUps.answer: Avoid microservices when: (1) Team is small — the operational overhead (separate deployments, monitoring, service discovery) requires dedicated DevOps capacity. A team of 3 developers can't afford to manage 10 services. (2) Domain is not well understood — drawing service boundaries requires knowing which parts of the domain change independently. Wrong boundaries create distributed monoliths (services that must always deploy together) — the worst of both worlds. (3) Consistency requirements are high — distributed transactions across services are painful. If your use case needs strong consistency across many entities, a monolith with transactions is simpler. (4) Performance is critical — network calls between services add latency. In-process function calls are orders of magnitude faster.

followUps.question: What is the strangler fig pattern for migrating from monolith?
followUps.answer: The strangler fig pattern migrates a monolith to microservices incrementally rather than a risky big-bang rewrite. You place a façade (API gateway or reverse proxy) in front of the monolith, then extract features one by one as new services. The façade routes requests: new services handle extracted features; the monolith handles the rest. Over time the monolith shrinks and the microservices grow — the monolith is "strangled" until it can be decommissioned. The key benefit: the system remains running throughout. Each extraction is a separate, contained migration. If a new service fails, you can route back to the monolith temporarily. This is how most real-world monolith-to-microservices migrations work in practice.

questions.tip: Start with a monolith. Microservices have enormous operational overhead (service discovery, distributed tracing, network partitions, eventual consistency). Only split when you have clear team/scaling boundaries that justify the complexity. Most startups shouldn't use microservices.

questions.question: What is an API Gateway and what does it do?
questions.answer:
An API Gateway is a single entry point for all client requests to a microservices system. Instead of clients calling individual services directly, they call the gateway which routes to the appropriate service.

What the gateway handles:

  • Routing — /api/orders → Order Service, /api/inventory → Inventory Service
  • Authentication — validate JWT once at the gateway instead of in every service
  • Rate limiting — throttle clients before requests reach services
  • SSL termination — HTTPS at the gateway, internal HTTP between services
  • Load balancing — distribute requests across service instances
  • Request aggregation — combine responses from multiple services into one (BFF pattern)
  • Circuit breaking — stop calling a failing service

Options in .NET ecosystem:

  • YARP (Yet Another Reverse Proxy) — Microsoft's .NET-native reverse proxy, highly configurable
  • Ocelot — popular open-source API gateway for .NET
  • Azure API Management — managed cloud gateway

In CS_PM_Service we used YARP as the API gateway — it handled routing and JWT validation centrally, so individual microservices only needed authorization (not authentication).

followUps.question: What is YARP?
followUps.answer: YARP (Yet Another Reverse Proxy) is a high-performance, .NET-native reverse proxy library from Microsoft. Unlike Nginx or HAProxy, YARP is configured in C# code and runs inside your ASP.NET Core process — you can use the full .NET middleware pipeline, DI, and logging. Routes are configured in appsettings.json or code and support hot-reload (no restart needed). YARP supports: request routing by path/host, load balancing across upstream clusters, session affinity, transforms (modify request/response headers), and health checks. It integrates with ASP.NET Core middleware so you can add rate limiting, authentication, or custom logic for specific routes. Used as the API gateway in CS_PM_Service to route and authenticate before forwarding to upstream services.

followUps.question: What is the BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern?
followUps.answer: BFF (Backend for Frontend) creates a dedicated API gateway/backend per client type — one for the mobile app, one for the web app, one for third-party partners. Each BFF aggregates and transforms microservice responses optimally for its specific client. The mobile BFF returns compact payloads (small screens, limited data); the web BFF returns richer data (full dashboard views). Without BFF, one generic API has to serve all clients — over-fetching for mobile, under-fetching for rich web UIs. BFF also allows each team to evolve their API contract independently. The trade-off: more services to maintain. In FolioTrack I would use BFF to have a web-optimized API (with all portfolio details) and a mobile-optimized API (stripped down for fast loading).

questions.tip: API Gateway is a single point of failure — deploy it with multiple instances behind a load balancer. YARP handles hot-reload of route configuration without restart, which is crucial for zero-downtime routing changes.

questions.question: What is event-driven architecture and how does it apply to microservices?
questions.answer:
In event-driven architecture, services communicate by producing and consuming events (messages) via a message broker (RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, Kafka). Services are decoupled — the producer doesn't know who consumes its events.

Key concepts:

  • Event producer — publishes events when something happens ("OrderPlaced", "PaymentProcessed")
  • Message broker — stores and routes events (Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ)
  • Event consumer — subscribes to events and processes them asynchronously
// Publisher (Order Service) — Azure Service Bus
public async Task PublishOrderPlacedAsync(Order order)
{
    var message = new ServiceBusMessage(
        JsonSerializer.SerializeToUtf8Bytes(
            new OrderPlacedEvent(order.Id, order.CustomerId, order.Total)));
    await _sender.SendMessageAsync(message);
}

// Consumer (Notification Service) — processes event async
public class OrderPlacedConsumer : BackgroundService
{
    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var processor = _serviceBusClient.CreateProcessor("order-placed-topic");
        processor.ProcessMessageAsync += async args =>
        {
            var evt = args.Message.Body.ToObjectFromJson<OrderPlacedEvent>();
            await _emailService.SendOrderConfirmationAsync(evt.CustomerId, evt.OrderId);
            await args.CompleteMessageAsync(args.Message);
        };
        await processor.StartProcessingAsync(ct);
    }
}

followUps.question: What is eventual consistency?
followUps.answer: Eventual consistency means that after an update, different parts of the system may temporarily show different data — but given enough time (no new updates), all replicas will converge to the same state. In event-driven microservices, a payment service processes a payment and publishes a PaymentProcessed event. The order service consumes it and updates the order status. Between those two moments, the payment service says "paid" but the order service still shows "pending" — that's eventual consistency. Systems must be designed to handle this: show stale data gracefully, avoid making decisions based on data from other services that may be stale, and use compensating transactions if things go wrong. The trade-off for eventual consistency is high availability and partition tolerance (CAP theorem).

followUps.question: What is the Outbox pattern?
followUps.answer: The Outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem: if you save to DB and then publish an event, a crash between those two steps loses the event. The fix: write the event to an Outbox table in the same database transaction as your business data. A separate background job (or the Transactional Outbox relay) polls the Outbox table and publishes events that haven't been sent yet. Once published, the row is marked as processed. This guarantees at-least-once delivery — the event is always published eventually, even if the process crashes. Combined with idempotent message handlers (safe to process twice), this achieves reliable messaging without distributed transactions. Libraries like MassTransit and Wolverine have built-in Outbox support for .NET.

questions.tip: Outbox pattern — solves the dual-write problem: saving to DB and publishing an event are not atomic. If you save and crash before publishing, the event is lost. Solution: write events to an Outbox DB table in the same transaction, then a background job publishes them reliably.

questions.question: What is the Circuit Breaker pattern and why is it important in microservices?
questions.answer:
The Circuit Breaker pattern prevents cascading failures in distributed systems. When a downstream service starts failing, the circuit breaker opens (stops sending requests) and returns a fallback response immediately — without waiting for timeouts that would cascade through the system.

Three states:

  • Closed — normal operation, requests flow through
  • Open — service is failing, requests short-circuit immediately (no calls made)
  • Half-Open — trial state: allow a few requests through to test if service recovered

Implementation with Polly:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IInventoryService, InventoryService>()
    .AddStandardResilienceHandler(options =>
    {
        // Retry: 3 attempts with exponential backoff
        options.Retry.MaxRetryAttempts = 3;
        options.Retry.Delay = TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(200);
        options.Retry.BackoffType = DelayBackoffType.Exponential;

        // Circuit Breaker
        options.CircuitBreaker.FailureRatio = 0.5;    // open if 50% fail
        options.CircuitBreaker.SamplingDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10);
        options.CircuitBreaker.BreakDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30);
    });

In CS_PM_Service, when the payment gateway was down, the circuit breaker opened immediately — preventing the entire order service from hanging with 30-second timeouts on every request.

followUps.question: What is Polly?
followUps.answer: Polly is the standard resilience library for .NET. It provides retry, circuit breaker, timeout, fallback, and bulkhead policies composable via a fluent API. In .NET 8, Polly v8 is integrated with Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience — use AddStandardResilienceHandler() which configures retry + circuit breaker + timeout with sensible production defaults in one call. For custom policies, wrap in a ResiliencePipeline: new ResiliencePipelineBuilder().AddRetry(...).AddCircuitBreaker(...).Build(). Polly is non-negotiable for any service calling external HTTP APIs — without retry and circuit breaker, a transient failure in one service propagates as cascading failures through the entire system.

followUps.question: What is bulkhead isolation?
followUps.answer: Bulkhead isolation limits the resources (threads, connections) one service can consume so failures in one part don't starve the entire system. Named after ship bulkheads — watertight compartments that prevent one hull breach from sinking the ship. In Polly: AddConcurrencyLimiter(maxConcurrentCalls: 10, queueSize: 5) — at most 10 concurrent calls to the downstream service; 5 can queue; additional requests are rejected immediately with a fallback. Without bulkhead: a slow downstream service causes all threads in the calling service to block waiting, exhausting the thread pool and making the calling service unresponsive. With bulkhead: only the isolated thread pool for that dependency is affected; the rest of the application continues normally.

questions.tip: Polly is the standard resilience library for .NET. In .NET 8, use AddStandardResilienceHandler() (from Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience) which configures retry + circuit breaker + timeout with sensible defaults. Don't roll your own retry logic.

questions.question: What is Clean Architecture and how does it relate to microservices?
questions.answer:
Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin) organizes code into concentric layers where inner layers don't know about outer layers:

  • Domain (innermost) — entities, value objects, domain events. Pure C# — no external dependencies.
  • Application — use cases (commands/queries), interfaces (IRepository), DTOs. Depends only on Domain.
  • Infrastructure — EF Core, HTTP clients, email senders. Implements Application interfaces.
  • Presentation (outermost) — controllers, Razor views. Depends on Application.
MyApp.Domain/          # Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events
  └── Entities/
  └── ValueObjects/
MyApp.Application/     # Use Cases, Interfaces, DTOs
  └── Portfolios/
      └── Commands/
      └── Queries/
  └── Interfaces/
MyApp.Infrastructure/  # EF Core, SMTP, HTTP, etc.
  └── Persistence/
  └── Email/
MyApp.Api/             # Controllers, Middleware, DI wiring

Dependency Rule: Source code dependencies always point inward. Domain knows nothing about EF Core or HTTP. This makes the domain testable in isolation and infrastructure replaceable.

Clean Architecture works at any scale — each microservice can be a Clean Architecture app internally.

followUps.question: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle in Clean Architecture?
followUps.answer: In Clean Architecture, the Dependency Inversion Principle is implemented structurally: the inner Application layer defines interfaces (IPortfolioRepository, IEmailSender), and the outer Infrastructure layer provides the implementations (EfPortfolioRepository, SmtpEmailSender). The Application layer depends on the abstraction (interface) it defines, not on the concrete infrastructure. The Infrastructure layer depends on (implements) the Application's interfaces — so the dependency arrow points inward (infrastructure depends on application, not the reverse). This keeps the domain and application layers free of framework dependencies, making them testable in isolation with fake implementations.

followUps.question: Clean Architecture vs Vertical Slice Architecture?
followUps.answer: Clean Architecture organizes by technical layer — Domain, Application, Infrastructure, Presentation as separate projects. Every feature touches all layers. Works well for domain-rich applications with complex business rules shared across features. Can become hard to navigate as the app grows — adding one feature requires touching 4 projects. Vertical Slice Architecture organizes by feature — each feature (Create Portfolio, List Holdings) has its own folder containing its command, handler, validator, endpoint, and DTOs. No shared layers. Easy to find all code for a feature. Better for teams because features are independent — one developer owns one slice. Trade-off: shared infrastructure code (DbContext, email) still needs a common project. Many modern .NET apps use Vertical Slices with MediatR; large domain-centric apps benefit from Clean Architecture's strict layering.

questions.tip: Vertical Slice Architecture is an alternative — organize by feature (Portfolio creation, Portfolio listing) rather than technical layers. Each feature has its own command, handler, validator, and query in one folder. Scales better with teams than the horizontal Clean Architecture layers.

questions.question: What is Docker and how do you containerize an ASP.NET Core application?
questions.answer:
Docker packages your application and all its dependencies into a container that runs identically on any machine — dev laptop, CI server, Azure. The classic 'it works on my machine' problem disappears. I've been using Docker in every project for the last 3 years and it genuinely changed how we deploy.

Core concepts:

  • Image — a read-only blueprint (your app + runtime + OS layer). Built from a Dockerfile.
  • Container — a running instance of an image. You can run many containers from one image.
  • Registry — storage for images. Docker Hub, Azure Container Registry (ACR), GitHub Container Registry.
  • Docker Compose — run multi-container apps (app + DB + Redis) together with one command.

Dockerfile for an ASP.NET Core app (multi-stage build):

# Stage 1: Build
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 AS build
WORKDIR /src
COPY ["MyApp/MyApp.csproj", "MyApp/"]
RUN dotnet restore "MyApp/MyApp.csproj"
COPY . .
WORKDIR "/src/MyApp"
RUN dotnet publish "MyApp.csproj" -c Release -o /app/publish

# Stage 2: Runtime image (smaller -- no SDK)
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 AS runtime
WORKDIR /app
EXPOSE 8080
COPY --from=build /app/publish .
ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "MyApp.dll"]

The multi-stage build is important — the final image only contains the runtime, not the 700MB SDK. Result: a small, secure image.

docker-compose.yml for local dev:

version: '3.8'
services:
  api:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "5000:8080"
    environment:
      - ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development
      - ConnectionStrings__Default=Server=db;Database=MyApp;User=sa;Password=YourPass123!
    depends_on:
      - db
      - redis

  db:
    image: mcr.microsoft.com/mssql/server:2022-latest
    environment:
      - SA_PASSWORD=YourPass123!
      - ACCEPT_EULA=Y
    ports:
      - "1433:1433"

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
# Run everything
docker-compose up -d

# View logs
docker-compose logs -f api

# Rebuild after code change
docker-compose up -d --build api

followUps.question: What is the difference between Docker and Kubernetes?
followUps.answer:
Docker runs individual containers. Kubernetes orchestrates many containers across many machines — it's a container management platform.

Think of it this way: Docker is like having trucks. Kubernetes is like having a logistics company that decides how many trucks to run, restarts broken trucks, routes traffic between them, scales up more trucks during busy periods, and does rolling deployments of new truck models without stopping service.

Kubernetes adds: auto-scaling (scale to zero, scale to 100 based on CPU/traffic), self-healing (restart crashed containers), rolling deployments with zero downtime, service discovery, load balancing between pods, secrets management, health probe integration.

For small apps, Docker Compose on a VM or Azure App Service for Containers is enough. For large-scale microservices, you need Kubernetes (AKS on Azure, EKS on AWS).

questions.tip: .dockerignore matters. Always have a .dockerignore file excluding bin/, obj/, .git, node_modules, etc. Otherwise COPY . . sends gigabytes of unnecessary files to the Docker build context, making builds very slow.

questions.question: What is gRPC and when would you use it instead of REST?
questions.answer:
gRPC is a high-performance, contract-first RPC framework from Google. Instead of REST's text-based JSON over HTTP/1.1, gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (Protobuf) for serialisation and runs over HTTP/2. I've used it for internal service-to-service communication where performance and strong typing mattered more than browser compatibility.

Why gRPC is faster than REST:

  • Protobuf — binary serialisation, 3-5x smaller payload than JSON, faster to serialise/deserialise.
  • HTTP/2 — multiplexing (multiple requests over one connection), header compression, server push.
  • Strongly typed contracts — .proto files define the schema; code is generated in both client and server languages. No mismatches, no manual mapping.

Setting up gRPC in ASP.NET Core:

// orders.proto
syntax = "proto3";
package orders;

service OrderService {
    rpc GetOrder (GetOrderRequest) returns (OrderReply);
    rpc StreamOrders (StreamOrdersRequest) returns (stream OrderReply); // server streaming
    rpc CreateOrder (CreateOrderRequest) returns (OrderReply);
}

message GetOrderRequest { int32 order_id = 1; }
message OrderReply {
    int32 id = 1;
    string status = 2;
    double total = 3;
}
// Server -- implement the generated abstract class
public class OrderGrpcService : OrderService.OrderServiceBase
{
    public override async Task<OrderReply> GetOrder(
        GetOrderRequest request, ServerCallContext context)
    {
        var order = await _db.Orders.FindAsync(request.OrderId);
        return new OrderReply { Id = order.Id, Status = order.Status };
    }
}

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddGrpc();
app.MapGrpcService<OrderGrpcService>();

// Client -- generated strongly-typed client
var channel = GrpcChannel.ForAddress("https://orders-service:5001");
var client = new OrderService.OrderServiceClient(channel);
var reply = await client.GetOrderAsync(new GetOrderRequest { OrderId = 1 });

gRPC streaming — where it really shines:

// Server streaming -- server pushes multiple messages to client
public override async Task StreamOrders(
    StreamOrdersRequest request,
    IServerStreamWriter<OrderReply> responseStream,
    ServerCallContext context)
{
    await foreach (var order in _db.Orders.AsAsyncEnumerable())
    {
        await responseStream.WriteAsync(new OrderReply { Id = order.Id });
        await Task.Delay(100); // simulating real-time data
    }
}

REST vs gRPC — when to use which:

  • REST — public APIs, browser clients, third-party consumers, human-readable debugging needed, simple CRUD.
  • gRPC — internal microservice-to-microservice calls, high-throughput data pipelines, real-time streaming, polyglot environments (Go service calling .NET service), mobile clients needing small payloads.

followUps.question: What is grpc-web and why is it needed?
followUps.answer: Standard gRPC requires HTTP/2 with full control over HTTP headers — something browsers can't do directly via the Fetch API or XMLHttpRequest. grpc-web is a modified protocol that wraps gRPC over HTTP/1.1 in a way browsers can consume. You add a grpc-web proxy (Envoy or the Grpc.AspNetCore.Web middleware) on the server side. The browser calls the proxy using grpc-web; the proxy translates to regular gRPC for the backend. This lets you use gRPC from Angular/React frontends. Honestly though, for browser clients I usually stick with REST unless the performance need is proven — the proxy adds complexity.

questions.tip: gRPC requires HTTP/2. If you're deploying behind a proxy or load balancer, make sure it supports HTTP/2 end-to-end. Some older load balancers don't — they terminate HTTP/2 and downgrade to HTTP/1.1, breaking gRPC silently.

questions.question: What is SignalR and when would you use it over regular REST polling?
questions.answer:
SignalR is ASP.NET Core's real-time communication library. It lets the server push data to connected clients without the client having to ask. Before I used SignalR, I had a dashboard that polled every 5 seconds — it was hammering the API and still felt laggy. SignalR eliminated both problems.

How SignalR works:

SignalR automatically chooses the best transport available:

  • WebSockets — true full-duplex, persistent connection. Preferred when available.
  • Server-Sent Events (SSE) — server-to-client only, one-directional, over HTTP.
  • Long Polling — fallback for environments where WebSockets aren't supported.

Setting up SignalR:

// Hub -- your real-time endpoint
public class OrderHub : Hub
{
    // Server method clients can call
    public async Task JoinOrderGroup(string orderId)
        => await Groups.AddToGroupAsync(Context.ConnectionId, $"order-{orderId}");

    public async Task LeaveOrderGroup(string orderId)
        => await Groups.RemoveFromGroupAsync(Context.ConnectionId, $"order-{orderId}");

    public override async Task OnDisconnectedAsync(Exception ex)
    {
        // cleanup
        await base.OnDisconnectedAsync(ex);
    }
}

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddSignalR();
app.MapHub<OrderHub>("/hubs/orders");

// Push from any service -- inject IHubContext
public class OrderService
{
    private readonly IHubContext<OrderHub> _hub;

    public async Task UpdateOrderStatusAsync(int orderId, string status)
    {
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync();

        // Push update to all clients watching this order
        await _hub.Clients
            .Group($"order-{orderId}")
            .SendAsync("OrderStatusUpdated", new { orderId, status });
    }
}

JavaScript client:

const connection = new signalR.HubConnectionBuilder()
    .withUrl("/hubs/orders")
    .withAutomaticReconnect() // auto-reconnect on disconnect
    .build();

connection.on("OrderStatusUpdated", (data) => {
    console.log(`Order ${data.orderId} is now ${data.status}`);
    updateUI(data);
});

await connection.start();
await connection.invoke("JoinOrderGroup", orderId);

When SignalR vs REST polling:

  • Use SignalR when: live dashboards, order tracking, notifications, chat, collaborative editing, stock prices, live sports scores — anything where the server initiates the update.
  • Use REST polling when: updates are infrequent, clients are not always connected, infrastructure doesn't support WebSockets (some proxies don't), or simplicity outweighs real-time needs.

Scale-out with Redis backplane:

// Multiple servers -- Redis backplane keeps all servers in sync
builder.Services.AddSignalR()
    .AddStackExchangeRedis(config["Redis:ConnectionString"]);

followUps.question: What is the difference between WebSockets and SignalR?
followUps.answer: WebSockets is the low-level browser/HTTP protocol for persistent bidirectional connections. SignalR is a higher-level abstraction on top of WebSockets (and other transports). SignalR adds: automatic transport fallback (WebSockets → SSE → Long Polling), connection management and reconnection, hub/method model (vs raw messages), group management, authentication integration, and scale-out via Redis/Azure Service Bus backplane. For most apps, use SignalR — it handles all the edge cases. Use raw WebSockets only if you need maximum control or minimal payload overhead, like a trading terminal.

questions.tip: SignalR in Azure: If you're on Azure, consider Azure SignalR Service — it offloads connection management from your app servers, handles scale-out automatically, and supports millions of concurrent connections without you managing a Redis backplane.

questions.question: What is CI/CD and how do you set it up for a .NET application?
questions.answer:
CI/CD automates the process of building, testing, and deploying your code. Before we had a pipeline, deployments were manual, error-prone, and took 2 hours. With CI/CD it's 10 minutes and fully automated. It's one of those things that feels optional until you've experienced it, then you can't go back.

CI (Continuous Integration):

Every push to the repo triggers an automated pipeline that builds the code and runs all tests. If tests fail, the build fails and you get notified. Nobody merges broken code.

CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment):

Automatically deploy to an environment (staging, production) after CI passes. Delivery = automated up to a manual approval gate. Deployment = fully automated, no human needed.

GitHub Actions pipeline for ASP.NET Core:

# .github/workflows/ci-cd.yml
name: CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Setup .NET
        uses: actions/setup-dotnet@v4
        with:
          dotnet-version: '8.0.x'

      - name: Restore dependencies
        run: dotnet restore

      - name: Build
        run: dotnet build --no-restore --configuration Release

      - name: Run tests
        run: dotnet test --no-build --configuration Release --verbosity normal
            --collect:"XPlat Code Coverage"

      - name: Publish
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        run: dotnet publish --configuration Release -o ./publish

      - name: Deploy to Azure Web App
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
        uses: azure/webapps-deploy@v2
        with:
          app-name: 'my-app-name'
          slot-name: 'staging'
          publish-profile: ${{ secrets.AZURE_WEBAPP_PUBLISH_PROFILE }}
          package: ./publish

Azure DevOps pipeline (YAML):

# azure-pipelines.yml
trigger:
  branches:
    include: [main]

pool:
  vmImage: 'ubuntu-latest'

steps:
- task: UseDotNet@2
  inputs:
    version: '8.x'

- script: dotnet restore
  displayName: 'Restore'

- script: dotnet build --configuration Release
  displayName: 'Build'

- script: dotnet test --configuration Release --logger trx
  displayName: 'Test'

- task: DotNetCoreCLI@2
  displayName: 'Publish'
  inputs:
    command: publish
    publishWebProjects: true
    arguments: '--configuration Release --output $(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)'

- task: AzureWebApp@1
  displayName: 'Deploy to Azure'
  inputs:
    azureSubscription: 'MyServiceConnection'
    appName: 'my-dotnet-app'
    package: '$(Build.ArtifactStagingDirectory)/**/*.zip'

What a good pipeline includes:

  • Restore → Build → Unit Tests → Integration Tests → Publish → Deploy to staging → Run smoke tests → Deploy to production (with approval gate).
  • Branch strategy: feature branches → PR to main → CI runs → merge → CD to staging → manual approve → CD to prod.
  • Secrets management: never put connection strings in pipeline YAML — use GitHub Secrets, Azure Key Vault, or pipeline variable groups.

followUps.question: What is a blue-green deployment?
followUps.answer:
Blue-green deployment runs two identical production environments simultaneously: Blue (current production, serving live traffic) and Green (the new version, being tested). When green is verified, you switch the load balancer to route all traffic from blue to green. If something goes wrong, you flip back to blue instantly — zero downtime rollback.

In Azure App Service, this is called deployment slots: you deploy to the 'staging' slot, run warm-up and smoke tests, then do a slot swap — Azure atomically routes traffic from staging to production. The swap is instant and reversible.

# Azure CLI
az webapp deployment slot swap \
    --name my-app \
    --resource-group my-rg \
    --slot staging \
    --target-slot production

questions.tip: Fail fast principle: Put the fastest checks first (build, unit tests) and slowest last (integration tests, E2E tests). You want developers to get feedback in 2-3 minutes for common failures, not wait 20 minutes to find out a syntax error broke the build.

questions.question: What is Azure App Service? How do you deploy an ASP.NET Core app to it?
questions.answer:
Azure App Service is a fully managed PaaS (Platform as a Service) for hosting web applications, APIs, and mobile backends. You give it your code or container, Azure handles the OS, patching, scaling, load balancing, and SSL. I've deployed maybe a dozen projects to App Service — it's the fastest way to get a .NET app live in the cloud.

Key features I use regularly:

  • Deployment slots — staging and production environments with instant swap for zero-downtime deploys.
  • Auto-scaling — scale out to more instances based on CPU/memory/requests. Scale in during quiet periods.
  • Built-in SSL — free managed certificates for custom domains.
  • Managed Identity — your app can talk to Azure SQL, Key Vault, Storage without storing any credentials anywhere.
  • Application Insights integration — one click to enable full telemetry, performance monitoring, alerting.

Deployment options:

# 1. GitHub Actions (most common -- see CI/CD question)
# Uses azure/webapps-deploy action

# 2. Azure CLI
az login
az webapp up \
    --name my-dotnet-app \
    --resource-group my-rg \
    --runtime "DOTNETCORE|8.0"

# 3. dotnet publish + zip deploy
dotnet publish -c Release -o ./publish
cd publish && zip -r ../app.zip .
az webapp deployment source config-zip \
    --name my-dotnet-app \
    --resource-group my-rg \
    --src app.zip

Configuration best practices:

// App Service auto-injects environment variables as configuration
// In Azure Portal: Configuration → Application Settings
// These override appsettings.json

// In code -- reads from env vars set in App Service config
var connStr = builder.Configuration["ConnectionStrings:Default"];

// For secrets -- use Key Vault references in App Settings
// Value: @Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://myvault.vault.azure.net/secrets/DbPassword)
// App Service resolves it automatically with Managed Identity

App Service Plan tiers (choose wisely for cost):

  • Free/Shared — dev/test only, shared infrastructure, no custom domain SSL, no deployment slots.
  • Basic — dedicated VMs, custom domains and SSL, no auto-scale.
  • Standard — auto-scale, deployment slots (5), daily backups. Good for most production apps.
  • Premium — more power, VNet integration, more slots. For high-traffic or compliance requirements.

followUps.question: What is Azure Functions and when would you use it instead of App Service?
followUps.answer:
Azure Functions is serverless — you write individual functions triggered by events (HTTP request, timer, Service Bus message, Blob storage upload, Cosmos DB change feed). You pay per execution, not per hour of a running server. Cold start time is the trade-off.

Use Azure Functions when: You have event-driven workloads (process an image when uploaded to Blob Storage, send an email on a timer, react to a queue message). Short-lived operations that don't need to be always-on. Cost-sensitive workloads with unpredictable traffic — you pay per execution not per server.

Use App Service when: You have a long-running web app or API that needs persistent connections, WebSockets (SignalR), warm startup time requirements, or complex routing. Always-on with predictable traffic.

// Azure Function example -- HTTP trigger
public class OrderProcessingFunction
{
    [Function("ProcessOrder")]
    public async Task Run(
        [ServiceBusTrigger("orders-queue", Connection = "ServiceBus")] string message,
        FunctionContext context)
    {
        var order = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Order>(message);
        await _orderService.ProcessAsync(order);
    }
}

questions.tip: Use Managed Identity everywhere in Azure. Instead of storing connection strings with passwords in App Settings, use Managed Identity to authenticate to Azure SQL, Redis, Key Vault, Storage. No credentials to rotate, no secrets to accidentally commit.
